coredata.dc:title: Hybrids of carbon dots with subunit B of ricin toxin for enhanced immunomodulatory activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yawei
Liu, Wensen
Sun, Chengbiao
Zheng, Min
Zhang, Jianxu
Liu, Bin
Wang, Yan
Xie, Zhigang
Xu, Na
coredata.dc:description: Although Ricin toxin binding subunit B (RTB) can promote the activation of macrophages and modulate the cell-mediated immunity, its applications are severely limited due to the intrinsic properties of proteins, like poor stability and low efficacy of cellular uptake. In this work, the stable nanoparticles were prepared by supramolecular assembling of carbon dots (CDs) and RTB. The formed CDs-RTB possesses robust stability and can protect RTB against enzymatic hydrolysis. More importantly, CDs-RTB can promote macrophages proliferation, improve the generation of NO, IL-6 and TNF-α in RAW264.7 cells and increase the expression of mRNA, indicating the enhanced immunomodulatory activity of CDs-RTB. This work highlights the potential of using CDs as a simple and stable platform to assemble RTB and effectively promotes the application of RTB as the immunostimulant.
coredata.dcterms:subject: RTB
Carbon dots
Nanoparticle
Macrophage
Immunomodulatory activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs, RTB and CDs-RTB
Measurement of NO and detection of cytokine production

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Project Agreement for Science & Technology Development, Jilin Province
National Natural Sciences Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Sciences Foundation of China (No. 31372487 and No. 81773630 ), Project Agreement for Science & Technology Development, Jilin Province (No. 20150204033YY and No. 20180201004YY ). Author 1, Author 2 and Author 3 contributed equally to this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) CDs and (B) CDs-RTB. Insert: Size distribution of CDs and CDs-RTB in aqueous medium. (C) Absorption and (D) PL (excited at 360 nm) spectra of CDs, RTB and CDs-RTB.

Changes of diameter and PDI of CDs-RTB dispersed (A) in water over 7 days and (B) in PBS with FBS (10%) over 24 h measured by DLS. (C) SDS-PAGE analysis of CDs in protection of proteins from enzymatic digestion.

(A) Biocompatibility of CDs and (B) Cell viability of free RTB and CDs-RTB towards RAW264.7 cells at different concentrations after 24 h of incubation. CLSM images of RAW264.7 cells incubated with (C) CDs-RTB and (D) CDs with the same CDs concentration of 50 µg mL−1 for designated time at 37 °C, scale bars: 10 µm. Statistical analysis was made by ANOVA. The level of significance was set at probabilities of * p < 0.05 and *** p < 0.001. Error bars represent s.d. (n = 3).

Effects of RTB and CDs-RTB at different concentrations on the production of (A) NO, (B) TNF-α, and (C) IL-6 from RAW264.7 cells. RAW264.7 cells were treated with RTB and CDs-RTB (12.5, 25, 50 µg mL−1) or LPS (50 ng mL−1) for 24 h. Statistical analysis was made by ANOVA. The level of significance was set at probabilities of * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, and *** p < 0.001. Error bars represent s.d. (n = 3).

Effects of RTB and CDs-RTB at different concentrations on the mRNA expression of (A) iNOS, (B) TNF-α and (C) IL-6 in RAW264.7 cells. Cells were incubated with RTB and CDs-RTB (12.5, 25, 50 µg mL−1) or LPS (50 ng mL−1) for 24 h. Total RNA was isolated and the mRNA expression was examined by RT-PCR. Densitometric analysis shows the relative intensity of the bands: relative ratios vs. β-actin. Statistical analysis was made by ANOVA. The level of significance was set at probabilities of * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, and *** p < 0.001. Error bars represent s.d. (n = 3).
Schematic illustration of the preparation (a) and cellular uptake (b) of CDs-RTB.

head.ce:title: Hybrids of carbon dots with subunit B of ricin toxin for enhanced immunomodulatory activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Jilin Province for Zoonosis Prevention and Control, Institute of Military Veterinary Medicine, Academy of Military Medical Sciences, Changchun 130122, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry and Life Science School, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Jilin Province for Zoonosis Prevention and Control, Institute of Military Veterinary Medicine, Academy of Military Medical Sciences, Changchun 130122, PR China (N. Xu).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Jilin Province for Zoonosis Prevention and Control

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although Ricin toxin binding subunit B (RTB) can promote the activation of macrophages and modulate the cell-mediated immunity, its applications are severely limited due to the intrinsic properties of proteins, like poor stability and low efficacy of cellular uptake. In this work, the stable nanoparticles were prepared by supramolecular assembling of carbon dots (CDs) and RTB. The formed CDs-RTB possesses robust stability and can protect RTB against enzymatic hydrolysis. More importantly, CDs-RTB can promote macrophages proliferation, improve the generation of NO, IL-6 and TNF-α in RAW264.7 cells and increase the expression of mRNA, indicating the enhanced immunomodulatory activity of CDs-RTB. This work highlights the potential of using CDs as a simple and stable platform to assemble RTB and effectively promotes the application of RTB as the immunostimulant.

ce:section.ce:para: Ricin toxin, one of the most lethal toxins, is a heterodimeric protein toxin produced by the seeds of Ricinus communis [1,2]. This toxin consists of A and B chains that are linked by a disulfide bond. The B-chain of the ricin toxin (RTB) with the molecular weight of 34 kDa is a galactose-binding lectin protein that binds to the membrane of eukaryotic cell by interacting with cell-surface molecules, such as galactose and glycolipids [3,4]. Several reports have demonstrated that RTB can function as a carrier fused to other molecules [5–7], or act as a target for antibody-based intervention therapeutics against infectious disease [8]. Furthermore, our previous works have proved that RTB can promote the activation of macrophages and modulate the cell-mediated immunity [9,10]. However, the applications of RTB are limited due to the intrinsic properties of proteins, including poor stability in biological environments, low bioavailability, short half-life period and enzymatic hydrolysis [11–14]. To address these issues, two strategies are commonly used. One is modification of RTB, mainly including PEGylation [15,16] and recombinant fusion protein [17,18]. The other is to load RTB into the different matrixes, such as hydrogels [19–21], liposomes [22,23], inorganic nanoparticles [24,25] and carbon materials [26,27]. Although these methods could improve the efficacy of protein therapeutics, some new problems, such as complicated synthesis, cytotoxicity of carriers and reducing protein activity spring up [15,21,25]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop an effective, safe and simple delivery vector to meet these challenges.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a class of fluorescent materials, have attracted a great deal of interest due to their intriguing properties, for instance ultrasmall size, water solubility, photostability, cell membrane permeability and biocompatibility [28–31]. They can be easily obtained from graphite and organic molecules such as citric acid or glucose [32–34], and emit blue, green, and red fluorescence [35,36]. To date, CDs have been widely used in energy conversion [37,38], ion detection [39–41], bioimaging fields [42,43] and drug delivery [44,45]. For example, very recently, we have assembled CDs with EFGP for intracellular protein delivery [46]. Inspired by this work, we anticipate that CDs also could load RTB through supramolecular interactions and deliver them into cells.

In this work, RTB was assembled with CDs to form the hybrid nanoparticles as shown in Scheme 1 . The formed CDs and RTB (CDs-RTB) nanoparticles possess small size and robust stability. Moreover, CDs-RTB also could activate macrophages effectively and enhance immunomodulatory activity.

All chemicals and reagents were purchased from commercial sources and used without further treatment, unless indicated otherwise. d-Glucose was purchased from Beijing Chemical Works, and the purity was 99.5%. Tetraethylenepentamine was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., and the purity was 90%. RPMI-1640 medium and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Gibco Life Technologies (Carlsbad, CA, USA). LPS, trypsin and Hoechst 33258 staining were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). BCA kit was purchased from Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). NO detection kit was purchased from R&D Systems (Abingdon, UK). IL-6 and TNF-α ELISA kits were purchased from eBioscience (Vienna, Austria). Trizol reagent kit and M-MuLV reverse transcriptase were purchased from Invitrogen (Carlsbad, CA, USA). Ethidium bromide (EB) was purchased from KeyGen Biotech Co. Milli-Q water was collected from a Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA).

Size distributions of the nanoparticles were determined by DLS with a Zeta-sizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd.). The measurement was carried out at 25 °C and the scattering angle was fixed at 90°. Zeta potential measurement was performed using a Zeta-sizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd.). The measurement was carried out at 25 °C, and the model was smoluchowski, the F(Ka) value was 1.50, the viscosity was 0.8872, RI was 1.330, dielectric constant was 78.5. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken by a JEOL JEM-1011 (Japan) at the accelerating voltage of 100 kV. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded via a Shimadzu UV-2450 UV–vis scanning spectrophotometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were conducted on a LS-55 fluorophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs, RTB and CDs-RTB
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were prepared by means of a modified thermal pyrolysis route starting from tetraethylenepentamine and glucose according to our previous work [28,36]. Briefly, d-Glucose and tetraethylenepentamine were loaded into a beaker at an equivalent stoichiometric ratio. The transparent solution was heated to 125 °C and kept for 30 min, then heated to 180 °C and maintained for 30 min. The final reaction products were completely solubilized, and then subjected to dialysis against water to remove small molecules for 1 d. The resulting product was freeze-dried to obtain brown solid.

The RTB was acquired following the protocol that has been reported [9,10]. The pET28a-RTB recombinant plasmid was transformed into competent BL21 (DE3) strain. A single colony was picked from a selective plate, inoculated into LB medium containing 100 μg mL−1 ampicillin and cultivated overnight at 37 °C with shaking (160 rpm). Expression of recombination RTB protein was induced with isopropyl-β-d-thiogalactopyranoside (IPTG) at a final concentration of 0.1 mM for 12 h and the cells were pelleted by centrifugation at 5000g for 20 min at 4 °C. The bacterial cell pellets were lysed by ultrasonicate. Inclusion bodies were harvested, washed, and solubilized in 6 M urea containing a mixture of denaturant and salt. The suspension was stored at 4 °C overnight and then centrifuged at 10,000g for 20 min. The supernatant, after filtering through a 0.45 mm filter (Millipore, Bedford, USA), was purified by Ni-NTA agarose (Qiagen, Hilden, Germany) affinity chromatography. The purified protein was then refolded by dialysis in phosphate-buffered saline in a diminishing concentration of urea. Then, Detoxi-Gel Endotoxin Removing Gel was used to remove the LPS from the RTB protein.

For a typical synthesis of CDs-RTB, 30 mg of CDs was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water; on the other hand, the equal weight of RTB was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water (pH 7.0). Under shaking, the aqueous of RTB was added into the solution of CDs rapidly, and the mixed solution was shook for overnight at 4 °C. After that, the free CDs and RTB in solution was separated by gel filtration chromatography. The sample was loaded onto a 1.6 × 90 cm Sephadex G-75 column pre-equilibrated with 20 mM PBS (pH 7.4) to separate CDs-RTB and RTB.

The role of CDs in protecting proteins from enzymatic cleavage was studied by trypsin digestion analysis. Briefly, trypsin aqueous solution (0.125%) was introduced into each free RTB or CDs-RTB samples with the same RTB concentration, and the enzyme reaction was performed at 37 °C for 6 h. All samples were mixed with loading buffer and heated to 100 °C for 10 min, and then analyzed by SDS-PAGE to detect the amount of remainder proteins.

RAW264.7 macrophages cells were purchased from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China) and cultured in RPMI-1640 medium supplemented with 10% FBS, 100 U mL−1 penicillin and 100 U mL−1 streptomycin at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 and 95% air. When the cells reached 80% confluence, the cells were subcultured by replacing the culture medium and the adherent cells were treated with trypsin, and seeded into new cell culture bottles to be incubated.

The effects of RTB and CDs-RTB on the viability of RAW264.7 cells were assessed using the MTT assay. Briefly, RAW264.7 cells in the logarithmic phase were plated at a density of 1 × 105 cells mL−1 into 96-well plates and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in 5% CO2 incubator. At the end of the treatment, cells were incubated with 100 μL of MTT (0.5 mg mL−1) at 37 °C for 4 h. Then, 100 μL of DMSO was added to each well. Absorbance of each well was detected at 450 nm using a microplate reader (Model 550; Bio Rad Laboratories, Inc., Hercules, CA, USA). The relative cell viability was expressed as the ratio of RTB or CDs-RTB treated cells to that of control cells.

Cellular uptakes by RAW264.7 cells were examined using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). RAW264.7 cells were seeded in 24-well culture plates (a sterile cover slip was put in each well) at a density of 5 × 105 cells per well and allowed to adhere for 24 h. After that, the cells were treated with CDs-RTB for 0.5 h, 2 h and 4 h at 37 °C, respectively, and then the supernatant was carefully removed and the cells were washed three times with PBS. Subsequently, the cells were fixed with 500 μL of 4% formaldehyde in each well for 20 min at room temperature, washed twice with PBS again, and stained with Hoechst 33258 staining solution (25 μg mL−1) for 10 min at room temperature. Then, the slides were mounted and observed with a CLSM (Zeiss LSM 780).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of NO and detection of cytokine production
ce:section.ce:para: The RAW264.7 cells were seeded into 6-well plates and incubated overnight. Cells were treated with RTB or CDs-RTB for 24 h. LPS (2.5 mg mL−1) was used as a positive control. NO generation was determined using Total Nitric Oxide and Nitrate/Nitrite Assay Kits, with nitrite production measured as the absorbance at 540 nm. The expression of IL-6 and TNF-α in the culture supernatant were measured by an ELISA kit following the manufacturer's instructions. Absorbance at 450 nm for standards and samples were measured using a NanoDrop ND-1000 spectrophotometer (NanoDrop Technologies, Wilmington, DE, USA).

RAW264.7 cells were seeded in 6-well plates treated with RTB or CDs-RTB for 24 h. The total cellular RNA was isolated from RAW264.7 cells using a Trizol reagent kit according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Total RNA (1 µg) was reverse-transcribed using M-MuLV reverse transcriptase. The following primers were used for PCR amplification: iNOS, forward: 5′-CTGCAGCACTTGGATCAGGAAG CTG-3′ and reverse: 5′-GGGAGTAGCCTGTGTGCACCTCGAA-3′. TNF-α, forward: 5′-TTCTGTCTACTGAACTTCGGGGTGATCGGTCC-3′, reverse: 5′-GTA TGAGATAGCAAATCGGCTGACGGTGTGGG-3′. IL-6, forward: 5′-TTCCCTACT TCACAAGTC-3′, reverse: 5′-ACTAGGTTTG CCGAGTAG-3′. β-actin, forward:5′-GTTGTCTCCTGCGACTTCA-3′, reverse: 5′-GGTGGTCCAGGGTTTCTTA-3′. PCR was performed on Applied Biosystems GeneAmp ® PCR System 9700 (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). PCR reactions were performed for 30 cycles with denaturation at 94 °C for 30 sec, annealing at 60 °C for 30 sec and extension at 72 °C for 60 sec. The PCR products were analyzed by electrophoresis on 1.5% agarose gels stained with ethidiumbromide. The relative quantification of mRNA expression was performed using β-actin as an internal control to normalize the gene expression for the PCR templates.

The mixture of CDs and RTB were shook for overnight at 4 °C to form the hybrids of CDs-RTB. The crude products were purified by column chromatography to remove CDs and free RTB (Fig. S1). As revealed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis, the as-synthesized CDs have the uniform size of about 3.8 nm (Fig. 1 A), and CDs-RTB possess average size of 225.6 nm with spherical shape (Fig. 1B). The RTB loading efficiency was calculated to be about 81.7% by a standard BCA protein assay method (Fig. S2). The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, RTB and CDs-RTB were shown in Fig. 1C, the maximum absorptions of CDs and RTB are at 277 nm and 269 nm, respectively, while the CDs-RTB shows the boarder and stronger absorption peak at around 274 nm, validating the co-assembly between CDs and RTB. In their PL spectra (Fig. 1D), the maximum emission of CDs is at 474 nm under an excitation of 360 nm. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-RTB was weaker than that of CDs due to the assembly of CDs with RTB. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were conducted to further study the assembly between CDs and RTB. As shown in Fig. S3, the vibration bands at 1409, 1607 and 3415 cm−1 demonstrate the presence of the respective aromatic amine and the aromatic skeleton of CC, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, respectively. As compared with CDs, new peaks at 1034 and 1217 cm−1 and other vibration bands in the fingerprint region are assigned to the stretching vibration of RTB. In addition, the bands at 1607 and 3415 cm−1 in CDs shift to 1599 and 3407 cm−1, respectively, indicating that there are strong interactions between CDs and RTB. Meanwhile, we compared the zeta potential values of CDs, RTB and CDs-RTB (Fig. S4). The zeta potentials of CDs, RTB and CDs-RTB are +17.6 mV, −21.5 mV and −12.1 mV respectively, confirming the electrostatic interaction between CDs and RTB.

The stability of CDs-RTB was investigated in various aqueous media. As shown in Fig. 2 A, all of the CDs-RTB in aqueous solution could keep their hydrodynamic diameter for a week, implying the high stability of CDs-RTB in water. Furthermore, the hydrodynamic diameters were monitored in PBS (pH 7.4) containing 10% FBS at 37 °C for 24 h, the size distribution of CDs-RTB also did not change significantly (Fig. 2B), indicating that no aggregation and dissociation occurred during 24 h of incubation. In addition, we investigated the stability of CDs-RTB in the presence of protease in vitro. Free RTB and CDs-RTB were both incubated with trypsin at 37 °C with shaking for 2 h, and the samples without trypsin were used as the control. The sodium dodecyl sulphate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) is shown in Fig. 2C. After treatment for 2 h, almost no protein was digested in the CDs-RTB samples treated with trypsin, which is similar with the CDs-RTB samples without protein enzyme digestion. In contrast, the free RTB was completely digested. Furthermore, no obvious changes in the size and size distribution of CDs-RTB were observed after trypsin hydrolysis (Fig. S7). These results illustrate that the hybrids of CDs and RTB could protect RTB from enzymatic degradation. This protection could ensure that RTB maintain their biological functions before reaching the target sites, which is very important for protein delivery.

Macrophages play an important role in both innate and adaptive immunity [47,48]. After induced by inflammatory stimulator, macrophages become activated and release lots of bioactive factors to initiate defense responses [49–51]. RAW264.7 cells are commonly accepted as a tool to investigate the molecular mechanisms of macrophages involved in regulating immunity [52]. In our previous work, we have proved that RTB can promote the activation of macrophage [9,10]. Herein, we studied the cellular proliferation of CDs-RTB and RTB towards RAW264.7 macrophages by a standard 3-(4,5-dimethyl-thiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay using a blank culture medium without samples as a negative control. Firstly, before CDs were used for drug carriers, cellular biocompatibility experiments were also carried out by MTT assay towards RAW264.7 cells. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the cell viability is very high in the varying concentration range, even at a high concentration of up to 100 μg mL−1, indicating the as-obtained CDs have good biocompatibility and can act as safe nanocarriers for drug delivery. Furthermore, we found that both RTB and CDs-RTB with the concentrations from 12.5 to 100 μg mL−1 can promote the macrophages proliferation (Fig. S8), while CDs-RTB exhibits higher proliferation ability than free RTB (Fig. 3B), suggesting the advantages of CDs-RTB nanoparticles. The possible reason may come from the suitable size of nanoparticles for effective endocytosis. Several studies have demonstrated that nanoscale size plays an important role in the activation of immune response [53–57]. In general, compared with free protein of several nanometers, larger particles (200–600 nm) which have a comparable size to pathogens can be easier recognized by antigen presenting cells and preferentially taken up by macrophages for induction of immune response [58–60]. Therefore, we hypothesize that spherical CDs-RTB nanoparticles (226.7 nm) can be internalized efficiently, and subsequently promote macrophages proliferation.

We studied the cellular uptake of CDs-RTB by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). RAW264.7 cells were incubated with CDs-RTB (50 μg mL−1) for 0.5, 2 and 4 h. The cell nuclei was visualized by using a blue channel for Hoechst 33258 (a nucleus staining dye), and a green channel for CDs-RTB. As shown in Fig. 3C, after incubation with CDs-RTB, the strong green fluorescence in cytoplasm is clearly observed, indicating that CDs-RTB can pass across the cell membrane into the cytoplasm. And the fluorescence intensity increases obviously from 0.5 h to 4 h, indicating that the CDs-RTB has a sustained cellular uptake and accumulation in macrophages. Furthermore, CDs-RTB also show stronger fluorescence compared with CDs at the same concentration (Fig. 3D). These results validate that the as-prepared CDs-RTB nanoparticles can be engulfed more efficiently by macrophages, which leads to the enhanced cellular proliferation indirectly.

It is well-known that macrophages participate in immune responses by releasing inflammatory factors such as NO and proinflammatory cytokines, including TNF-α and IL-6 [50,61,62]. Indeed, the NO produced by inducible Nitric Oxide Synthase (iNOS) can not only enhance the activity of macrophages, but also play an important role in the regulation of various immune responses [63,64]. IL-6 and TNF-α, which are important cytokines involved in immune responses against pathogens, can enhance various functional responses and contribute to the systemic effects of inflammation [65,66]. Therefore, the current experiments were designed to investigate the immunomodulatory effects of CDs-RTB on RAW264.7 macrophages by analyzing the production and expression of NO, TNF-α and IL-6, and the cells treated with lipopolysaccharide (LPS, 50 ng mL−1) was used as the positive control. Firstly, we detected the release of NO by using Griess reagent. As shown in Fig. 4 A, a minimum amount of NO was released when RAW264.7 cells were exposed to medium alone, whereas a concentration-dependent increase in NO production was observed after incubation with increasing amounts of RTB or CDs-RTB for 24 h. Furthermore, CDs-RTB can activate macrophages to release more amount of NO than that of free RTB. In addition, the content of TNF-α and IL-6 in supernatant of RAW264.7 cells treated with different concentrations of RTB or CDs-RTB were detected using ELISA reagent kits. The production of TNF-α and IL-6 followed a tendency similar to that of NO release (Fig. 4B and C). Untreated RAW264.7 cells secreted a basal level of TNF-α and IL-6, whereas RTB or CDs-RTB treatment resulted in remarked increase in IL-6 and TNF-α protein levels in a concentration-dependent manner. Meanwhile, the production of TNF-α and IL-6 stimulated by CDs-RTB were higher than those of RTB group at the same condition. These results demonstrate that CDs-RTB can effectively activate macrophages and release more amount of NO and cytokines than free RTB, which contributes to immunostimulating activities.

Because of the prominent role of iNOS, IL-6 and TNF-α in the maturation and function of macrophages, the expression levels of these mediators in RAW264.7 cells treated with RTB or CD-RTB were further investigated by reverse transcriptase-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR), and the cells treated with LPS (50 ng mL−1) was used as the positive control. As shown in Fig. 5 , untreated RAW264.7 cells expressed lower levels of inflammatory factors (iNOS, IL-6, TNF-α), whereas the mRNA expression levels of all those inflammatory factors significantly increased after treatment of RTB and CDs-RTB, indicating that RTB and CDs-RTB can induce the mRNA expression of iNOS and those cytokines in a concentration-dependent manner. More importantly, we also found that the mRNA expression levels of the above genes in the CDs-RTB group were higher than those in the RTB group at the same concentration of RTB, which is consistent with the results of NO and cytokines production detection. All these results further indicate that CDs-RTB can activate macrophages efficiently.

In summary, we developed a facile and efficient approach for the preparation of CDs-RTB nanoparticles. The as-synthesized CDs-RTB not only exhibits superior physical stability in physiology conditions, but also can prevent the proteins from enzymatic hydrolysis efficiently and deliver RTB into the cytoplasm. Moreover, compared with free RTB, CDs-RTB nanoparticles can promote macrophages proliferation, release more amount of NO, IL-6 and TNF-α, and increase the mRNA expression levels of all these inflammatory factors more effectively, indicating the enhanced immunomodulatory activity of CDs-RTB. The superior performance of CDs-RTB promotes the application of RTB as the immunostimulant effectively, which has been rarely reported so far. Therefore, this work provides insight into rational design of efficient supermolecular hybrids based on fluorescent CDs for protein delivery systems, and may open up new opportunities for both fundamental and applied research of CDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Sciences Foundation of China (No. 31372487 and No. 81773630), Project Agreement for Science & Technology Development, Jilin Province (No. 20150204033YY and No. 20180201004YY). Author 1, Author 2 and Author 3 contributed equally to this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.03.108.
